articles.headline: Abolition of Slavery -- Albany people of colour resolve to celebrate emancipation on the 5th of July
articles.summary: Proceedings of a meeting of the people of colour of Albany, held at the African meeting-house on March 27, 1827, to consider celebrating the abolition of slavery in New York State (effective July 4, 1827). Benjamin Lattimore, Sen. was called to the chair and Anthony Olcott appointed secretary; the Rev. Mr. Paul (Nathaniel Paul) delivered a short address contrasting the present state and prospects of the people of colour with those of their forefathers. Resolutions expressed gratitude to Almighty God and public benefactors, and -- because July 4 is the day 'the National Independence of this country is recognized by the white citizens' -- fixed the celebration for the 5th of July. A committee of arrangement of twelve was appointed: Adam Blake, Thomas Alcott, Richard Thompson, William Hyres, Robert Harrison, Benjamin Lattimore Jr., John Jackson, Asher Root, Anthony Olcott, Daniel Maynard, Peter Hallenbeck, and Henry Jackson. Signed Benj. Lattimore, Sen., Ch'n; Anthony Olcott, Sec'ry.
articles.notes: The editors elsewhere in the issue correct the New-York Enquirer: the Albany celebration was to be on July 5th, not the 4th. Motions credited to the son of Mr. Lewis Topp and to Benjamin Lattimore, jun.

articles.headline: African Improvement Society of New-Haven -- meeting at the North Church
articles.summary: Reprinted from the Connecticut Journal: the African Improvement Society of New-Haven, formed 'for the improvement of the moral, intellectual, and religious condition of the African population' of that city, held a meeting on a Friday evening at the North Church in company with a large collection of citizens; a resolution approving the society's object and deeming it 'worthy of the united patronage of the citizens of New-Haven' was unanimously adopted. The meeting was addressed 'in a very able manner' by Rev. S. S. Jocelyn, Prof. Silliman, Prof. N. W. Taylor, and T. Dwight. The reprinted remarks argue that 'it is not so much the colour as the character' that causes odium, urging that colored residents learn useful sciences and trades.
articles.notes: The Freedom's Journal editors promise 'Remarks in our next' -- delivered as the April 27 editorial dissenting from the character-over-colour thesis.

articles.headline: The Revolt in Texas
articles.summary: Editorial on the 'Republic of Fredonia' revolt in the Mexican province of Texas, attributing it to the new Mexican law prohibiting the importation of slaves (or abolishing slavery altogether). Slave-holders from the United States who had gone to Texas expecting to amass fortunes by the sinews and traffic of slaves, finding 'their craft in danger,' set up a government of their own 'in the true spirit of Governor Troup'; the neighboring Indians sided with the Mexicans and the revolt collapsed. The editors praise the new republics of North and South America -- Colombia (last slave to be emancipated within the present year), Peru, and Mexico -- as setting an example on slavery, while 'the United States, where the torch of liberty was first kindled... are cherishing in their bosom nearly 2,000,000 of wretched slaves.'

articles.headline: Hayti, No. I -- From the Scrap Book of Africanus
articles.author: Africanus (pseud.)
articles.summary: First installment of a serialized history of Hayti, reprinted from the Christian Watchman, opening with the Declaration of Independence's self-evident truths and proposing to trace 'the past and present condition of a people, who have bravely burst asunder the galling chains of slavery.' This installment covers Columbus's 1492 discovery of the island, the character of the native population, the Spanish extermination of the natives, and the French settlement under Duparquet and D'Ogeron; by 1788 the French part was 'considered by far the most valuable of the Antilles,' with a population upwards of 600,000 and annual produce over $25,000,000.
articles.notes: Series continues in subsequent issues (No. II on April 27, No. III on May 4).

articles.headline: Mutability of Human Affairs
articles.summary: Editorial essay arguing from history that 'the ancient Ethiopians were considered as a blameless race' honored by the gods (quoting Homer), that the Egyptians and Ethiopians were of one colour with a striking similarity of features and equally civilized, and tracing the rise and fall of Egypt, Rome, and Constantinople to show that mutability attends all nations. Asks whether 'the day may not come when our people shall be duly considered in the scale of nations, and respected accordingly,' and invokes the early African Christians -- Tertullian, Cyprian, Origen, Lactantius -- asking 'is it not time to enquire after the descendants of men who have hazarded their lives to preserve the faith of the Gospel pure and unadulterated?'
articles.notes: One of the paper's signature early essays vindicating African history against prejudice.

articles.headline: Committee notice -- call for the general meeting of the People of Colour (Wm. Hamilton, Thos. L. Jennings, Peter Bane)
articles.summary: Notice signed by WM. HAMILTON, THOS. L. JENNINGS, and PETER BANE as a Committee, issued 'agreeably to resolution' of a meeting of the People of Colour, referencing the Mutual Relief Hall in Orange-street -- the call that produced the large April 23, 1827 general meeting on commemorating the abolition of domestic slavery in New York State (reported in the following issue).
articles.notes: OCR of this notice is fragmentary; the three signers and the Orange-street location are clear.

articles.headline: Cheap Clothing Store, No. 218 South Sixth-street, Philadelphia (Asa B. Peterson)
articles.summary: Advertisement of Asa B. Peterson's Cheap Clothing Store at No. 218 South Sixth-street, Philadelphia: a large assortment of gentlemen's ready-made wearing apparel, new and second-handed, with tailoring carried on in its various branches 'on the cheapest terms.' A recurring Philadelphia advertisement in Freedom's Journal.
articles.notes: Philadelphia Black business advertising in the New York paper -- evidence of the paper's Philadelphia readership alongside agent Francis Webb.

articles.headline: United States' Scouring and Steam Sponging, No. 122 North Third-street, Philadelphia (John H. Smith)
articles.summary: Advertisement of John H. Smith's scouring and dressing establishment at No. 122 North Third-street (above Race), Philadelphia: dressing of gentlemen's coats and pantaloons 'on a different plan from that of the Dyers,' restoring clothes to an appearance equal to new, warranted for three months; also ladies' habits and merino shawls. States he was 'legally bred to the business' with competent knowledge of dressing and cleaning cloths by steam sponging. Keeps new and second-handed clothes on hand for cash or barter.
articles.notes: Recurring Philadelphia advertisement; the same issue also carries New York advertisers Thomas L. Jennings (110 Nassau-st, cast-off clothes) and the St. Philip's Church school notice.

articles.headline: People of Colour (Concluded) -- against gradualism and colonization palliatives
articles.author: S. F. D.
articles.summary: Conclusion of a reprinted Christian Spectator essay arguing that neither the generosity of slave-holders nor colonization schemes will end slavery: 'Nothing but freedom itself will fit a man to be free.' Cites Jefferson's 1814 letter to Gov. Coles, argues slavery curses every department of national prosperity (answering McDuffie of South Carolina), that emancipation of bondmen in every recorded instance has been instantaneous rather than gradual, that palliatives 'are like opium in a fever,' and that the measures taken 'must be aimed at the root': immediate legal recognition of slaves as human beings, protection of persons and rights, establishment of marriage, admissibility as witnesses, and mixed juries (de medietate linguae) in suits involving a black party. Signed S. F. D.

articles.headline: St. Philip's Church collection for the Auxiliary New-York Bible and Common Prayer Book Society -- exchange of resolutions and letter of Rev. Peter Williams
articles.author: Peter Williams (Rector)
articles.summary: On Sunday April 8, 1827 a sermon was preached at St. Philip's Church by the Rt. Rev. Bishop Hobart and a collection of $35.54 taken for the Auxiliary New-York Bible and Common Prayer Book Society, whose managers had for years gratuitously supplied the church's Sunday schools and indigent members with Bibles and prayer books. The society's board (resolutions of April 12, 1827, signed Richard Oakley, Recording Sec. pro tem.) presented the church two elegantly bound octavo Common Prayer books for the chancel with a gilt inscription. The Vestry's grateful reply, dated New-York April 23, 1827, is signed PETER WILLIAMS, Rector.
articles.notes: Documents the standing of St. Philip's (African Episcopal) Church and Rev. Peter Williams Jr. in 1827 New York.

articles.headline: Meeting of the People of Colour -- commemorating the abolition of domestic slavery in New York (Mutual Relief Hall, April 23, 1827)
articles.summary: A very large and respectable meeting of the People of Colour convened in the Mutual Relief Hall, Orange-street, on April 23, 1827, to consider the best plan for commemorating the abolition of domestic slavery in New York State. William Hamilton was called to the chair and Thomas L. Jennings appointed secretary. Resolutions (printed on p.3): to celebrate the 4th of July next 'as a Jubilee of emancipation from Domestic Slavery'; that the different religious congregations have prayers and thanksgivings in their churches that morning, with an oration by a suitable person; that a committee of seven make arrangements; and -- to express gratitude to the Legislature and avoid 'the least tendency to disorder' -- 'we will therefore abstain from all processions in the public streets on that day.' Signed W. HAMILTON, Chairman; T. L. JENNINGS, Secretary.
articles.notes: The famous 'no processions' resolution for New York's Emancipation Day. The omitted fourth resolve (committee of seven) was supplied by T. L. Jinnings' letter in the May 4 issue.

articles.headline: Editorial on the New-Haven society and the condition of the people of colour -- Philadelphia property statistics
articles.summary: The editors welcome the formation of the New-Haven African Improvement Society ('worthy of a Washington or a Franklin') but dissent from the Connecticut Journal's opinion that odium attaches to character more than colour. As evidence of industry and frugality they cite the city of Philadelphia, where 'according to the return of the assessors in 1822 there were 219 estates held by coloured men, and valued at 114,939 dollars [OCR: 1143939], and worth perhaps twice that sum,' noting respectable mechanics whose successful industry gained them wealth and esteem, and that 'prejudice deprives them of the privilege of learning trades... and patronage, after they have obtained them.' Argues that some brethren 'in point of character, information and competency, may justly be compared with some of the whites, and yet their colour is made the mark of reproach.'
articles.notes: Key early statistical claim about Black property-holding in Philadelphia.

articles.headline: Baltimore Justice!! -- trial of Woolfolk, the slave trader, for beating Benjamin Lundy
articles.summary: Report of the trial of Woolfolk, the slave trader, for beating Benjamin Lundy, editor of the Genius of Universal Emancipation. Chief Justice Brice, pronouncing sentence, observed 'he had never seen a case in which the provocation for a battery was greater' -- that Woolfolk was engaged in a trade sanctioned by the laws of Maryland, that the trade was 'beneficial to the state, as it removed a great many rogues and vagabonds,' and that Lundy 'had received no more than a merited chastisement.' Obliged nonetheless to fine him something, the Court fined Woolfolk one dollar and costs.
articles.notes: Notorious episode: Austin Woolfolk's assault on abolitionist editor Benjamin Lundy, effectively excused by the Baltimore bench.

articles.headline: The Curse of Canaan
articles.summary: Reprinted from the New-York Observer: a careful refutation of the common claim (as in Dr. Collyer's letter) that African slavery fulfills Noah's prophecy against Canaan (Gen. ix. 25-27). Argues the prophecy was distinctly fulfilled in the Canaanites themselves; that extending it to all of Ham's descendants founders on Nimrod, Egypt, and Ethiopia; and that even if it did include African slavery, 'a thing's being foretold has nothing to do with the question whether it is right or wrong' -- God also foretold Israel's slavery in Egypt and punished the enslavers. Notes that hearers of the prophecy-defense have wrongly taken it as justifying slavery.
articles.notes: An extended answer to the era's most common scriptural defense of slavery.

articles.headline: Toussaint L'Ouverture -- biographical sketch (serialized)
articles.summary: Serialized sketch of Toussaint L'Ouverture from the Quarterly Review's article on the 'Past and Present state of Hayti' (via the Catskill Recorder), introduced by the editors as proof 'that the negro is not, in general, wanting in the higher qualifications of the mind.' Covers his birth in slavery about 1745 on the Count de Noe's plantation, his learning to read under M. Bayou de Libertas, his rescue of his master's family in the 1791 insurrection, his rise from physician of the forces under Jean Francois to Commander-in-Chief, the release of Governor Leveaux ('this Spartacus, predicted by Raynal'), his diplomacy with the British General Maitland, and (in the May 11 continuation) his answer to Hedouville -- 'Your ship, sir, is not large enough for a man like me' -- his honorable treatment of Maitland against Roume's treachery, and (concluded May 18) his betrayal and death in a French dungeon, 'the floor being actually covered with water. Thus did this great and good man perish.'

articles.headline: Letter of Thomas L. Jinnings -- the omitted fourth resolve and the committee of seven
articles.author: Thomas L. Jinnings
articles.summary: Letter to the editors dated New-York, May 1, 1827, supplying an important omission in the published proceedings of the April 23 General Meeting of the People of Colour: the fourth resolve, 'That the chairman nominate the said committee,' which being carried, John Marander, Thomas L. Jinnings, Thomas Sipkins, Soup Robertson [OCR uncertain; possibly 'Sous' or another given name], William Miller, Henry Scott, and Moses Blue were nominated and approved as the committee of seven for the July 4th emancipation celebration.
articles.notes: Names the full arrangements committee for New York's first Emancipation Day celebration.

articles.headline: Hayti, No. III -- From the Scrap Book of Africanus
articles.author: Africanus (pseud.)
articles.summary: Third installment of the Hayti series: the first prelude of the Revolution among the colonists; the news of the Bastille's fall reaching Cape Haytien in 1789; the People of Colour's hopes after the Declaration of Rights (August 17, 1789); Oge and Raymond sailing from France in July 1790; the revolt of the People of Colour in which Oge, taken prisoner, 'suffered the most excruciating tortures, being broken on the wheel'; the National Assembly's May 15, 1791 vote granting people of colour the privileges of French citizens; and the general slave revolt of August 1791 in which 'twenty thousand men, burning with wrongs innumerable, determined upon freedom or death.' The author argues the revolt of the People of Colour 'opened the eyes of the slaves... that they too had certain unalienable rights.'

articles.headline: Abolition of Slavery in the State of New-York
articles.summary: Editorial anticipating July 4, 1827: 'The time is at hand in which the friends of liberty will see the promise of God verified... and their laudable efforts result in the final abolition of slavery in this state.' Reflects on the delight such a day would have given 'Sharp, Benezet, Rush, and other distinguished worthies' [OCR garbled: 'Sarge, Benesett']; thanks Mr. Curtis and the worthy members of the New-York Manumission Society, who 'merit, and we trust, will ever receive, the warmest gratitude of the people of colour'; and suggests the abolitionists advise the emancipated on their future pursuits -- ideally agriculture -- lest, flocking uncultivated into the large cities, 'many of them will fall victims to temptation.'

articles.headline: Notice -- consecration of the African Free Methodist Society house, 136 Mulberry-street
articles.summary: Notice that the house designed for the use of the 'African Free Methodist Society,' situated at No. 136 Mulberry-street between Hester and Grand (opposite the African Free School), will be consecrated on the Lord's Day, May 6, 1827, at 2 o'clock P.M., with a sermon by the Rev. William Quinn.
articles.notes: Rev. William Quinn is very likely William Paul Quinn, the itinerant AME preacher and later fourth AME bishop long associated with Philadelphia.

articles.headline: Notice -- New-York Union Society removes to Mutual Relief Hall, 44 Orange-street
articles.summary: Notice that the 'New-York Union Society' will, after the first of May current, meet at the Mutual Relief Hall, No. 44 Orange-street, for the ensuing year.
articles.notes: Fixes the street number (44 Orange-street) of the Mutual Relief Hall, site of the April 23 general meeting.

articles.headline: Married in Philadelphia -- Nathaniel Adams of Washington, D.C. to Anne Allen, youngest daughter of Bishop Richard Allen
articles.summary: Marriage notice: 'In Philadelphia, on the 25th ult. [April 25, 1827] Mr. NATHANIEL ADAMS, of Washington, D. C. to Miss ANNE ALLEN, youngest daughter of the Rt. Rev. Richard Allen, Bishop of the African Methodist Episcopal Church.' The same column records New York marriages by Rev. Mr. Miller (Henry Davis of Va. to Miss Jane Low) and by Rev. B. Paul (John Anderson to Miss Joanna Cornish [OCR: Cornill]).
articles.notes: Direct documentation of Bishop Richard Allen's family in the Philadelphia database's core period.

articles.headline: Reward for kidnapped son of Mrs. Betsey Wallis, free woman of colour of Baltimore
articles.summary: Mrs. Betsey Wallis, a free woman of colour of Baltimore, offers a reward of $100 for the recovery of her son, John Wallis, missing since the 8th of March 1827; 'there is reason to believe that he has been kidnapped.' The same summary column reports that Col. Ward of Virginia died leaving about one hundred and fifty slaves free by his last will, with a tract of land on which a number are settled.
articles.notes: One of the paper's running documentation of kidnapping of free people of colour.

articles.headline: Philadelphia education society formed -- building for the reception of coloured youth (John Bowers, President; John Gloucester, Secretary)
articles.summary: Communication dated Philadelphia, April 28, 1827: 'A number of the respectable people of colour of this city, deeply impressed with the importance of education, have formed themselves into a society for the purpose of raising funds' to secure 'a suitable building, and furniture, for the reception of coloured youth.' The organization took place April 20, 1827, and the society adjourned to meet the second Wednesday in May at 8 o'clock P.M. in the hall occupied by the Second African Presbyterian congregation of Philadelphia, at which time the Rev. Jeremiah Gloucester is expected to deliver an address on education. Signed JOHN BOWERS, President; JOHN GLOUCESTER, Secretary.
articles.notes: Major Philadelphia item: an April 1827 Black education society with John Bowers presiding and a Gloucester as secretary; Rev. Jeremiah Gloucester was pastor of Second African Presbyterian. Prefaced by an essay on the knowledge of the world and the importance of educating youth.

articles.headline: Philanthropy of Miss Wright -- the Nashoba establishment
articles.summary: Reprinted from the Philadelphia Ariel: Miss Frances Wright has commenced an establishment at Nashoba in Tennessee 'with the benevolent design of affording an asylum to slaves,' assigning to ten trustees a tract of nearly nineteen hundred acres in perpetual trust for the benefit of the negro race. She holds that more is required of colonization societies than emancipating the body of the slave -- the mind must be informed and prepared for the enjoyment of liberty.

articles.headline: Madame Christophe -- a defense against the New-York Enquirer
articles.summary: The editors rebuke the New-York Enquirer's coarse description of the Ex-Queen of Hayti, widow of King Henry Christophe, as calumny. From 'a personal acquaintance with Madame Christophe for many years previous to and after she was elevated to the rank of Queen,' they testify that she always sustained the reputation of a good and virtuous wife, an affectionate mother, an amiable friend, and a hospitable and charitable lady, whose courtesy to foreigners (especially Americans) at the Cape was well known; that she has estates in Hayti from which Gen. Magny, Commandant of the North, remits the revenues; and they conjecture the foreign paragraph about a German prince's marriage may concern her eldest daughter. They regret 'that such misrepresentations should originate in the United States.'
articles.notes: The offending remark is attributed to the Editor of the N. York Enquirer 'from whom better things were expected' -- Mordecai Noah, a recurring antagonist.

articles.headline: Slavery in the West-Indies -- answer to the New-York Evening Post
articles.summary: Extended editorial answering the 'venerable Editor of the New-York Evening Post' (Mr. Coleman), who had palliated West-India slavery on the strength of Alexander Barclay's pro-planter volume answering Stephen. The editors dismiss planter testimony ('Just as well might Mr. C. summon a Jew to testify in favour of the Divinity of Christ'), and against the claim that few people of colour would not acknowledge they were better off under good masters, reply: 'We have been intimately acquainted with the free people of colour in most of the states... for ten years past; and we have never found a single individual who preferred a state of bondage to that of freedom.' As facts they cite a file of two Jamaica papers containing eighty-five advertisements for runaway slaves, most marked or scarred by punishment, quoting one for 'Bob, alias Robert Grant... marked M. B. on the right breast... and scars of punishment on the back.' Concluded in the May 18 issue's continuation of the argument against the assumed intellectual inferiority of the blacks.

articles.headline: Examples of Black genius and attainment -- Sancho, Fuller, Wheatley, Banneker, Derham, Vassa, Cuffee's contemporaries
articles.summary: A compiled catalogue (citing Gregoire and Rees) of persons of African descent whose lives refute the assumed intellectual inferiority of the blacks: Ignatius Sancho (letters published in England); Thomas Fuller of Alexandria, the calculating prodigy who corrected his questioners on leap years (as related by Dr. Rush); Belinda's 1782 petition to the Massachusetts legislature; the Methodist preacher Maddocks; 'Othello' of Baltimore (1789 essay against slavery); Cesar of North Carolina, poet; Ottobah Cugoano; Gustavus Vassa (Olaudah Equiano) and his Memoirs and 1789 parliamentary petition; Phillis Wheatley; Benjamin Banneker, whose almanacs were published in Philadelphia 1794-95; the son of Nimbana, king of Sierra Leone; James Derham, formerly a slave in Philadelphia, who became 'the most distinguished physician at New-Orleans' ('I learned more from him than he could expect from me' -- Dr. Rush); Toussaint L'Ouverture; and Christophe.
articles.notes: Part of the running series against the argument 'drawn from the assumed fact of the inferiority of the blacks in point of intellect' (opened by 'The surprising influence of prejudice' from the Abolition Intelligencer on p.1). James Derham's Philadelphia origin is directly relevant to the project.

articles.headline: A Man of Colour on Mr. Clay's Colonization Society speech -- Philadelphia meeting of nearly three thousand opposed
articles.author: A Man of Colour (pseud.)
articles.summary: Letter drawing attention to Henry Clay's speech before the last annual meeting of the Colonization Society at Washington: the society's increase 'cannot be viewed in any other light, than a desire to get effectually rid of the free people'; Clay avowed the society would have nothing to do with 'the delicate question of Slavery' and proposed removing six thousand free persons annually to keep down 'their alarming increase.' The writer disputes the Baltimore Memorial as unrepresentative ('at least two-thirds of the meeting dissented from it') and reports: 'At a meeting lately held in Philadelphia, of the most respectable people of colour, consisting of nearly three thousand persons, to take this subject into consideration, there was not one who was in favour of leaving this country; but they were all opposed to colonization in any foreign country whatever.' Also cites South Carolina's prohibition on free people of colour returning to the state, and a writer 'P.' in Poulson's paper of March 21 who 'speaks the sentiments of these people in Pennsylvania.'
articles.notes: Early direct testimony of mass Philadelphia opposition to colonization, consistent with the Bethel Church meetings.

articles.headline: African Free Schools in the United States
articles.summary: Editorial (continued on p.3 and 'To be Continued') inviting attention to the present state of the different African Free Schools: 'the future respectability of our people will eventually rest on the education which our children and youth now receive.' Laments that 'no door is open to receive the degraded children of Africa' in the seminaries founded for all other classes; reprobates the custom of extravagantly praising the most simple attainments of a person of colour as itself an obstacle; surveys the value of schools from the Pilgrim fathers, Scotland's parochial schools, Sparta, and Rome; and criticizes the imperfect, irregular education so far afforded ('Parrot-like they have acquired them, and parrot-like they forget them').
articles.notes: Also on p.2 is an extract from Rev. O. P. Hoyt's July 4, 1826 Potsdam address on slavery ('a cloud gathering over our country... heavy with vengeance'), predicting insurrection if slavery is not abolished and citing the Charleston plot.

events.name: Albany meeting of the people of colour on celebrating New York abolition
events.description: Meeting of the people of colour of Albany to consider celebrating the July 4, 1827 abolition of slavery in New York State; resolved to celebrate on the 5th of July (since the 4th is the white citizens' Independence Day) and appointed a committee of arrangement of twelve.
attendees: his son moved the first resolution
moved the committee resolution; committee of arrangement

events.name: African Improvement Society of New-Haven -- public meeting at the North Church
events.description: Public meeting of the African Improvement Society of New-Haven 'on Friday evening last' with a large collection of citizens; resolution of united patronage unanimously adopted. Date approximate (the Friday preceding the April 20 issue, as reprinted from the Connecticut Journal).

events.name: General Meeting of the People of Colour on commemorating the abolition of domestic slavery in New York
events.location: Mutual Relief Hall, 44 Orange-street, New York
events.description: Very large and respectable general meeting to plan the commemoration of the abolition of domestic slavery in New York State: resolved to celebrate July 4, 1827 as a Jubilee of emancipation, with church services and an oration but no public street processions; a committee of seven was nominated by the chair (its membership printed in the May 4 issue via T. L. Jinnings' correction letter). Called by a committee of William Hamilton, Thomas L. Jennings, and Peter Bane (notice in the April 20 issue).
attendees: Chairman; member of the calling committee
Secretary; committee of seven; member of the calling committee
committee of seven (OCR of given name uncertain)

events.name: Consecration of the African Free Methodist Society house, 136 Mulberry-street
events.location: No. 136 Mulberry-street (between Hester and Grand, opposite the African Free School), New York
events.description: Consecration of the house designed for the use of the African Free Methodist Society, at 2 o'clock P.M. on the Lord's Day, with a sermon by Rev. William Quinn.

events.name: Marriage of Nathaniel Adams and Anne Allen, daughter of Bishop Richard Allen
events.description: Marriage in Philadelphia of Mr. Nathaniel Adams of Washington, D.C., to Miss Anne Allen, youngest daughter of the Rt. Rev. Richard Allen, Bishop of the African Methodist Episcopal Church.
attendees: Rt. Rev., father of the bride

events.name: Organization of the Philadelphia society for the education of coloured youth
events.location: Philadelphia (adjourned meeting at the hall of the Second African Presbyterian congregation)
events.description: Organization of a society of respectable people of colour of Philadelphia to raise funds to secure a suitable building and furniture for the reception of coloured youth; adjourned to meet the second Wednesday in May 1827 at 8 P.M. in the hall occupied by the Second African Presbyterian congregation, where Rev. Jeremiah Gloucester was expected to deliver an address on education.
attendees: Rev., to deliver the address on education
